name: Send an email from buildingshultz@gmail.com
description: Solomon sends an email via the Gmail API (HTTPS, bypasses SMTP block). Used for outreach, replies, contact-form responses, and any other outbound email. Plain-text + auto-rendered HTML multipart. Recipient on a sensitive domain (bank / .gov / attorney / payment processor / healthcare) is refused at the executor level unless Jed pre-approved via acknowledge_sensitive:true.
trigger_examples: send an email to john@example.com — subject Hello, body Quick note about the deck
email marketing@kleintools.com about the Crusaders program
reply to that contact-form message via email
shoot an email to my supplier asking about the cedar order
prompt_template: Call executeTool('send_email', { to: {{to}}, subject: {{subject}}, body: {{body}}, cc: {{cc}}, reply_to: {{reply_to}} }). Telegram-confirm to Jed with one line: ✉️ Sent to {{to}} — {{subject}}.
escalation_conditions: Recipient address ends in .gov / .mil OR is on a known bank, attorney, payment processor, or healthcare domain → executor will refuse without acknowledge_sensitive; escalate to Jed before flipping that flag.
Email body promises money, makes a contractual commitment, accepts terms, or contains personal info about Tasia or the kids → escalate to Jed regardless of recipient.
Reply chain involves a complaint, refund request, legal letter, or upset customer → escalate so Jed reviews tone.

smoke_test.trigger_message: send an email to buildingshultz@gmail.com — subject Solomon dispatch smoke test, body Please ignore. Just verifying the dispatch template wires up.

notes: The executor enforces a sensitive-domain guard regardless of dispatch confidence — that's the load-bearing safety, not the template categories. The 'public_brand' category was deliberately NOT added so routine emails don't pay the Nathan-consult tax; brand-risk judgment lives in the few-shot anchors of the classifier and in the human escalation conditions above.
